data.sourceText:
“One Minutes” (15 per side)

Complete Consideration of H.R. 8800 – National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2027 (Rep. Rogers (AL) – Armed Services) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

This annual defense authorization bill passed out of the Armed Services Committee on a 44-12 vote. As passed out of committee, the bill aims to improve pay, housing, childcare, and healthcare for service members, military families, and civilian personnel. It authorizes a 7 percent pay raise for service members ranked E-5 and below, a 6% pay raise for service members ranked E-6 to O-3, and a 5% pay raise for service members ranked O-4 and above. The bill excludes the Basic Housing Allowance from a service member’s household income when determining eligibility for the Basic Needs Allowance, which provides supplemental income to low-income military families. It authorizes over $2 billion in new construction of barracks and dormitories, includes several provisions to expand access to childcare, and authorizes resources to local educational agencies with military-dependent students. It also authorizes no-cost dental coverage under TRICARE for members of the Reserve Component ranked E-1 through E-4, prohibits the Department of Defense (DOD) from eliminating services at Military Treatment Facilities, and establishes a pilot program to increase staffing at Military Treatment Facilities. 

During the full committee markup, HASC Democrats secured the inclusion of important provisions through amendments that protect the collective bargaining rights of DOD civilian employees, reverse Secretary Hegseth’s direction on the names of certain military installations, and authorize healthcare coverage for assisted reproductive technologies to support service members and their dependents. The amendments also require transparency and reporting on the Minab girls school bombing investigation and the costs of Trump’s war of choice with Iran, as well as the ongoing boat strikes and military operations in U.S. Southern Command’s area of responsibility.

However, the bill endorses President Trump’s request for $1.15 trillion in discretionary spending for the DOD, National Nuclear Security Agency, and other national security programs. The bill would also change the DOD’s name to the Department of War, which the Congressional Budget Office estimates could cost as much as $125 million. While the country faces threats and the DOD has true modernization needs, Ranking Member Smith believes these serious issues must be addressed in a more responsible manner – and certainly not on the back of massive cuts to critical domestic programs while at the same time cutting taxes that benefit the ultra-rich. The DOD needs to become more innovative and efficient in terms of developing capabilities to meet the nation’s national security needs in a more cost-effective manner. It is also crucial to develop a realistic national security strategy and approach that doesn’t require a $1.15 trillion discretionary budget request, a side-request for an additional $350 billion through a partisan budget reconciliation process, and an additional $67 billion sought through a supplemental budget request because the president started an unnecessary and unconstitutional war with Iran. 

With adoption of the Rule, the text of S. 1383 – SAVE America Act will be inserted into the bill upon final passage. House Republicans are choosing to yet again to make it more difficult for millions of Americans to vote even though there has been zero evidence of the widespread fraud the SAVE America Act purports to target.

Ranking Member Smith will oppose this bill.

The Rule, which was adopted yesterday, provides for no further general debate and made in order 320 amendments.

As of yesterday, the House has completed debate through Onder #27. Members may track consideration of amendments HERE.

H.Con.Res. 113 – Establishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2027 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2028 through 2036 (Rep. Arrington – Budget) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

Instead of working to pass policies that will lower costs for Americans, President Trump and Congressional Republicans have used the past two reconciliation processes to give billionaires massive tax breaks on the backs of working people and to give billions of dollars to ICE – a lawless agency that just this week shot and killed another individual. 

Now, Congressional Republicans are starting a third reconciliation process to use taxpayer dollars to pay for the administration's mistakes abroad while also investing in new ways to disenfranchise voters. Specifically, this budget resolution will spend up to $95 billion, with $73 billion to cover the cost of the unconstitutional Iran War, $12 billion in farm assistance largely needed because of President Trumps reckless tariff agenda as well as the war, and $10 billion to incentivize states to implement SAVE America Act policies.

For almost five months, President Trump has spent billions of dollars on a war with no end in sight. The president continues to insist that Iran wants to make a deal that never materializes – while Americans are facing gas prices that are thirty percent higher than before the war and food costs continue to soar. Instead of ending a war that Congress has already rejected, Congressional Republicans and President Trump are using this budget resolution to force everyday Americans to pick up the tab.

At the same time, Congressional Republicans are using this budget resolution to bribe states to implement the unpopular policies of the SAVE America Act. In exchange for funding, states could require American citizens to register to vote with an election official by providing, for the most part, (1) a passport, or (2) a birth certificate with a current photo identification. Alongside a few other uncommon documents, in person at an election office would be the only way for American citizens to register to vote. These restrictions would place an extreme burden on countless Americans, including our military, Native Americans, people who have changed their names (including tens of millions of American women), seniors, and more. The policies states could implement would hinder the right to vote – when it is already illegal under current law for noncitizens to register to vote or to vote in federal elections, and there has been zero evidence of the widespread fraud being alleged by President Trump and Congressional Republicans.

House Democrats are ready to deliver real solutions to working families as they continue to face the affordability crisis. Unfortunately, Congressional Republicans have decided to add almost $100 billion to the deficit in the name of putting America last to fund President Trump’s war of choice and make it harder to exercise the right to vote.

Ranking Member Boyle strongly opposes this resolution.

The Rule, which was adopted yesterday, provides for one hour of debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on the Budget or their respective designees.

H.R. 7008 – Stop Insider Trading Act (Rep. Steil – House Administration) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

This bill permits Members of Congress, as well as their spouses and dependent children, to continue to own and sell stocks, and even purchase certain securities and privately traded stocks. Additionally, this bill requires a narrow set of acceptable photo identification in order to cast a ballot in a federal election, and would functionally eviscerate mail voting by forcing Americans to include photocopies of their IDs alongside their mailed ballots.

In short, House Republicans are using this bill to pretend to address critical concerns – while failing to hold those in power responsible. While this bill does limit one type of security purchases by Members, spouses, and dependent children, trades can still be made in securities in non-public companies, non-security futures, and commodities. Members may also continue to sell stocks, although now with a waiting period and bolstered disclosure requirements. Additionally, this bill allows for trading in non-diversified investment funds concentrated in a Member's particular Congressional district or state – allowing bad actors to profit from area-specific information or through the appropriations process. And perhaps most glaringly, Members do not need to divest from any of their current holdings or put them in any kind of a blind trust. 

Additionally, by choosing not to address the president or vice president in this bill, House Republicans are once again ignoring the blatant and constant corruption displayed by the White House. President Trump and those around him continue to profit from access and inside information – while everyday Americans struggle to make ends meet. If House Republicans were serious about cracking down on abuses of power in the federal government, they would need to include the executive branch.

By jamming voter identification language into this bill, House Republicans are also bolstering President Trump’s efforts to subvert democracy. It is a solution in search of a problem. There is no evidence to support the president’s claims of widespread voter fraud and requiring voters to send a copy of their ID with their ballot would end vote by mail as we know it. In fact, Republican Governor of Ohio Mike DeWine recently vetoed a similar state proposal to require Ohioans to surrender copies of photo identification alongside their mail ballots, noting that such restrictions “would not discourage fraud, would not add any real security, and would create an additional and significant burden for Ohioans who vote by mail.” What is included in this bill is an attempt to burden American voters by needlessly restricting the types of acceptable identification required for voting, creating another avenue to pursue widespread voter suppression and sow doubt in the security and integrity of elections. 

House Democrats have filed a discharge petition that would bring the Restore Trust in Government Act to the Floor, which is a comprehensive Congressional stock trading ban bill – and includes the president and vice president. If House Republicans truly wanted to address political corruption, they could start by joining House Democrats in this effort.

Ranking Member Morelle strongly opposes this bill.

The Rule, which was adopted yesterday, provides for one hour of debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on House Administration or their respective designees.

Begin Consideration of H.Con.Res. 89 – Directing the President, pursuant to section 5(c) of the War Powers Resolution, to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities with Iran (Rep. Jayapal – Foreign Affairs) (Privileged Resolution) – VOTE YES

Possible Postponed Suspensions (3 votes)

  1. H.R. 8205 – Accelerating Access to Critical Therapies for ALS Reauthorization Act of 2026, as amended (Rep. Quigley – Energy and Commerce)
  2. H.R. 7283 – Ensuring Federal Purchasing Efficiency Act (Rep. Fallon – Oversight and Government Reform)
  3. H.R. 1722 – Billion Dollar Boondoggle Act, as amended (Rep. Miller-Meeks – Oversight and Government Reform)

“One Minutes” (15 per side)

H.Res. 1438 – Rule providing for consideration of H.R. 6955 – Main Street Capital Access Act; H.R. 9770 – Continuing Appropriations Act, 2027; H.R. 8800 – National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2027; H.Con.Res. 113 – Establishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2027 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2028 through 2036; H.R. 7008 – Stop Insider Trading Act; and H.R. 8884 – Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act – VOTE NO

Members are advised that the Rule adds the text of S. 1383 – SAVE America Act to the NDAA upon passage of the NDAA on the Floor.

H.R. 6955 – Main Street Capital Access Act (Rep. Hill – Financial Services) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

This bill purports to revitalize community banks and make access to capital easier for Main Street, but in reality, it is a package of almost entirely Republican bills that would empower Wall Street megabanks – at a time when they are making record-breaking profits and the Trump Administration is gutting the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) – with the most sweeping bank deregulation since before the 2008 global financial crisis.

House Democrats recently worked with House Republicans to enact into law a bipartisan housing bill that included a number of sensible reforms that will help community banks and credit unions better serve their communities. Unfortunately, House Republicans have decided to put forward a partisan package with little input from House Democrats that advances their own partisan agenda while hijacking bipartisan priorities. This time, they have done it in the interest of deregulating big banks in ways that will put Americans at financial risk.

Instead of learning lessons from previous financial crises, this bill would heighten the chances of another one. The bill would strip away supervision of bank management practices and give all banks more leeway to challenge virtually all regulatory actions. It would allow large banks to bypass vital safeguards that are meant to promote safety, enabling them to take unnecessary risks that can lead to financial harm to everyday Americans. President Trump tried this once before in 2018, with Congress and his regulators rolling back safeguards for regional banks, and a few years later, we had the fastest bank run and the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th largest bank failures in U.S. history. In the wake of the recent failure of Silicon Valley Bank and other banks like it, this bill would give President Trump’s regulators new tools to roll back safeguards for even these kinds of large banks and could lead to a 2008 type of financial crisis.

Consumers continue to struggle from Trump’s affordability crisis. Instead of curbing costly fees or strengthening consumer protections, this bill waters them down. Right now, the CFPB is effectively shut down by the Trump Administration. This bill goes even further by rolling back the application of a number of critical consumer protection laws to a rapidly growing number of banks while undermining the CFPB’s ability to issue new rules and enforce the law. In effect, it would leave consumers without a strong watchdog to protect their interests even after President Trump leaves office.

At a time when Americans are already experiencing financial hardships due to President Trump’s disastrous economic policies and war of choice with Iran, House Republicans are choosing to once again put Main Street at risk and are legitimizing President Trump’s efforts to gut the safeguards that have protected our economy and consumers. House Democrats stand ready to work on actual reforms that will support the community banks and credit unions Americans rely on every day.

Ranking Member Waters strongly opposes this bill.

The Rule will provide for one hour of debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on Financial Services or their respective designees.

H.R. 9770 – Continuing Appropriations Act, 2027 (Rep. Cole – Appropriations) (Subject to a Rule)

This CR funds the government through December 4, 2026. There is no funding for ICE, but the bill dropped a provision in the 2026 Homeland Security Appropriations Act that explicitly removed funding for Border Patrol. 

Ranking Member DeLauro opposes this bill.

The Rule will provide for one hour of debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on Appropriations or their respective designees.

Begin Consideration of H.R. 8800 – National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2027 (Rep. Rogers (AL) – Armed Services) (Subject to a Rule)

This annual defense authorization bill passed out of the Armed Services Committee on a 44-12 vote. As passed out of committee, the bill aims to improve pay, housing, childcare, and healthcare for service members, military families, and civilian personnel. It authorizes a 7 percent pay raise for service members ranked E-5 and below, a 6% pay raise for service members ranked E-6 to O-3, and a 5% pay raise for service members ranked O-4 and above. The bill excludes the Basic Housing Allowance from a service member’s household income when determining eligibility for the Basic Needs Allowance, which provides supplemental income to low-income military families. It authorizes over $2 billion in new construction of barracks and dormitories, includes several provisions to expand access to childcare, and authorizes resources to local educational agencies with military-dependent students. It also authorizes no-cost dental coverage under TRICARE for members of the Reserve Component ranked E-1 through E-4, prohibits the Department of Defense (DOD) from eliminating services at Military Treatment Facilities, and establishes a pilot program to increase staffing at Military Treatment Facilities. 

During the full committee markup, HASC Democrats secured the inclusion of important provisions through amendments that protect the collective bargaining rights of DOD civilian employees, reverse Secretary Hegseth’s direction on the names of certain military installations, and authorize healthcare coverage for assisted reproductive technologies to support service members and their dependents. The amendments also require transparency and reporting on the Minab girls school bombing investigation and the costs of Trump’s war of choice with Iran, as well as the ongoing boat strikes and military operations in U.S. Southern Command’s area of responsibility.

However, the bill endorses President Trump’s request for $1.15 trillion in discretionary spending for the DOD, National Nuclear Security Agency, and other national security programs. The bill would also change the DOD’s name to the Department of War, which the Congressional Budget Office estimates could cost as much as $125 million. While the country faces threats and the DOD has true modernization needs, Ranking Member Smith believes these serious issues must be addressed in a more responsible manner – and certainly not on the back of massive cuts to critical domestic programs while at the same time cutting taxes that benefit the ultra-rich. The DOD needs to become more innovative and efficient in terms of developing capabilities to meet the nation’s national security needs in a more cost-effective manner. It is also crucial to develop a realistic national security strategy and approach that doesn’t require a $1.15 trillion discretionary budget request, a side-request for an additional $350 billion through a partisan budget reconciliation process, and an additional $67 billion sought through a supplemental budget request because the president started an unnecessary and unconstitutional war with Iran. 

While Ranking Member Smith voted to advance the bill out of committee, he is likely to oppose final passage of the bill based on its support for an unprecedented $1.15 trillion in discretionary national security spending, while also closely monitoring the outcome of any potential poison pill amendments.

The Rule will provide for one hour of general debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on Armed Services or their respective designees and makes in order 320 amendments. Members may track consideration of amendments HERE.

Postponed Suspension (1 vote)

  1. H.R. 1118 – Value Over Cost Act of 2026, as amended (Rep. Donalds – Oversight and Government Reform)

Possible Postponed Suspensions (3 votes)

  1. H.R. 8205 – Accelerating Access to Critical Therapies for ALS Reauthorization Act of 2026, as amended (Rep. Quigley – Energy and Commerce)
  2. H.R. 7283 – Ensuring Federal Purchasing Efficiency Act (Rep. Fallon – Oversight and Government Reform)
  3. H.R. 1722 – Billion Dollar Boondoggle Act, as amended (Rep. Miller-Meeks – Oversight and Government Reform)
